nativeRange.habitat: Permanent lowland swamps and small, slow-moving coastal rainforest streams with soft, acidic, tannin-stained water and dense vegetation

diet.notes: Micropredator; generally does best on small live and frozen foods. Frozen daphnia, brine shrimp, bloodworms, and blackworms are commonly accepted and recommended for best color and conditioning. Tank-bred specimens have been reported to accept quality micro-pellets and flake food, but live or frozen foods are generally advised as the primary diet.

avoidWith: fin-nipping species (males have ornate tail extensions vulnerable to damage)
other male killifish in small tanks

summary: Colorful West African non-annual killifish with a distinctive lyretail and warm orange-to-gold body. Generally considered one of the more beginner-friendly killifish, though cooler water requirements and escape risk warrant some attention.
careNotes:
Non-annual killifish: unlike Nothobranchius and other annual killifish, Aphyosemion australe inhabits permanent water bodies (swamps and coastal streams) and does not undergo a seasonal diapause. Captive lifespans of 2–3 years are commonly reported; some sources indicate over 3 years is achievable in well-maintained conditions.

Cooler water requirement: this species is generally advised to be kept cooler than typical tropical fish — around 21–24°C. FishBase and the majority of specialist sources (SeriouslyFish, user husbandry notes) align on this range. TFH Magazine suggests up to 25–26°C (76–78°F) as acceptable, and SeriouslyFish lists a broader 21–32°C tolerance range, but the 32°C upper limit is inconsistent with the species' coastal rainforest origin and the consensus from other sources. Temperatures above 25°C are generally not recommended for long-term keeping.

Escape risk — tight lid mandatory: this species is a capable jumper and a secure, tight-fitting lid is considered essential. In smaller or shallow tanks the risk is particularly high.

Color morphs: several ornamental color strains are commonly available in the trade, including 'Gold' (orange-gold body with red spotting, the most widely kept), 'Orange' (deeper orange tones), and 'Chocolate' (brown-olive base with red spots). The wild type resembles the Gold strain. All share the same care requirements.

Color and peat: color intensity has been observed to improve with peat extract in the water (mimicking the natural blackwater biotope) and dimmer, indirect lighting. Spawning success also tends to improve in soft, slightly acidic conditions (pH 6.0–6.5 is commonly recommended for breeding).

Grouping: a pair (1M:1F) or a trio (1M:2F) is commonly advised. Males tend to be less aggressive toward each other than the related Fundulopanchax gardneri; multiple males can coexist in a well-planted tank with sufficient visual breaks, though males will display and occasionally chase each other. To reduce male harassment of females, keeping 2 females per male is generally recommended.

Water quality: soft, acidic water with low kH (0–5) is generally preferred. Gentle filtration — sponge filter or air-driven filter — is recommended to avoid excessive flow. Peat moss in the filter or in a breeding mop setup is beneficial for conditioning.

Related species note: compare with Fundulopanchax gardneri (Steel-blue Killifish), a sibling freshwater killifish in the family Nothobranchiidae. The gardneri tends to be slightly hardier, tolerates a wider pH and temperature range, and males can be more aggressive toward each other; the lyretail is generally considered the gentler and slightly more delicate of the two.
breedingNotes:
Breeding is generally considered moderate in difficulty and accessible for aquarists new to killifish. This species is a plant/mop spawner ('egg-hanger'): eggs are individually deposited among fine-leaved plants, Java moss, or synthetic spawning mops. Eggs can be incubated in water (hatching in approximately 10–20 days at 21–24°C) or removed and stored semi-dry on peat moss (hatching in around 18 days when re-wetted). Daily egg production of roughly 10–20 eggs over a 2-week period is commonly reported during active spawning.

Fry are relatively large at hatching and have been reported to accept newly hatched Artemia (brine shrimp) nauplii immediately, though infusoria or commercial micro-foods for the first day or two may improve survival. Adults will consume eggs and fry; separating eggs or raising fry in a dedicated grow-out container is generally advised. Small, frequent water changes (approximately 10–20% every 2–3 days) are commonly recommended for fry growth.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name, family/order, adult size (6.0 cm TL), native range (Angola, Gabon, Cameroon, Congo), temperature (21–24°C), pH (6.0–7.0), and hardness (5–12 dH). SeriouslyFish's 32°C max temperature was considered an outlier inconsistent with FishBase and user-provided data; the FishBase range of 21–24°C was adopted.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Top 5 Killifish
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (~6 cm / 2.5 in), diet (bloodworms, brine shrimp, Vibra Bites), tank size guidance (10–20 gallon / 38–76L for pairs/trios), and general behavior notes.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Admiring Aphyosemion australe
additional.notes: Third source used for lifespan (2–3 years), temperature preference (25°C / 76–78°F cited; slightly warmer than FishBase consensus), pH guidance (6.5–7.0 preferred), and husbandry notes on diet and behavior. Note: TFH states the species is not prone to jumping, which disagrees with SeriouslyFish and general killifish guidance; the majority-source position (escape risk = high) was retained.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer/hanger in spawning mops or fine-leaved plants. Eggs incubate 10–20 days in water. Fry accept Artemia nauplii immediately. See breedingNotes for full details.
